module.title: STRING PATTERNS
module.subtitle: format · find · match · gsub · gmatch
module.theory:
## What this sector is about
Text is data. To read a log line, pull a name out of a packet, or clean up dirty input, you need to **search inside strings** and **rebuild them**. Lua gives you a tiny, fast pattern engine for exactly this.

> WARNING — Lua patterns are **NOT regular expressions (regex)**. They look similar but the rules differ. `%d` works, but `\d` does NOT. Forget PCRE here — learn the few Lua rules below and you are done.

## string.format — build a clean string
`string.format` fills in blanks (called **specifiers**) with your values, like a fill-in template.
~~~lua
print(string.format("%s lvl %d", "NEO", 7))  -- NEO lvl 7
print(string.format("%.2f credits", 3.5))     -- 3.50 credits
~~~
The common specifiers: **%s** = a string, **%d** = a whole number, **%.2f** = a decimal with 2 places.

> INTEL — `%d` needs an integer. `string.format("%d", 3.5)` will error. Use `%.0f` if you want to round a decimal, or `%d` only on real integers.

## What a pattern is — the character classes
A **pattern** is a mini-description of "what kind of text I am looking for". The building blocks are **classes** (each matches ONE character):
~~~text
%d  one digit        0-9
%a  one letter       A-Z a-z
%s  one space        space, tab, newline
%w  one letter OR digit (alphanumeric)
.   any one character at all
~~~
A **capital** class means the opposite: **%D** = not a digit, **%A** = not a letter, **%S** = not a space.

## Quantifiers — how many
By itself a class matches exactly one character. Add a symbol after it to repeat:
~~~text
%d+   one OR MORE digits   (greedy: grabs as many as it can)
%d*   zero or more digits
%d-   zero or more, but as FEW as possible (lazy)
~~~
So `%d+` matches `"42"` as the whole thing, not just `"4"`.

## string.find — where is it?
`string.find(s, pattern)` returns the **start and end positions** of the first match, or **nil** if nothing matched. Positions are **1-indexed** (the first character is 1, not 0).
~~~lua
local i, j = string.find("id=A7", "%d+")
print(i, j)  -- 5   5   (the single digit "7" sits at position 5)
~~~
A plain word with no special characters just finds that literal text:
~~~lua
print(string.find("hello world", "world"))  -- 7   11
~~~

> WARNING — beginners expect index **0** for "first character". In Lua the first character is at index **1**, and `find` returns `nil` (not -1, not 0) when there is no match. Always test `if string.find(...) then`.

## Anchors — ^ start and $ end
Put **^** at the FRONT of a pattern to mean "must match at the very start of the string". Put **$** at the END to mean "must reach the very end".
~~~lua
print(string.find("abc", "^a"))   -- 1  1  (starts with a)
print(string.find("abc", "^b"))   -- nil  (does not start with b)
print(string.match("4242", "^%d+$"))  -- 4242 (the WHOLE string is digits)
~~~
`^%d+$` is the classic "is this string made of only digits?" check.

> INTEL — `^` only anchors when it is the FIRST character of the pattern. Anywhere else it is just a literal `^`. Same idea for `$` only at the very end.

## string.match — give me the text
`string.find` tells you WHERE. `string.match` gives you the matched **text itself** (or **nil** if no match).
~~~lua
print(string.match("id=A7", "%d+"))  -- 7
~~~

## Captures — pull pieces out with ( )
Wrap part of a pattern in **parentheses** to **capture** it. `match` then returns just those pieces, in order.
~~~lua
local user, host = string.match("neo@matrix", "(%a+)@(%a+)")
print(user, host)  -- neo   matrix
~~~
Without captures you get the whole match. With captures you get exactly the pieces you wrapped.

## string.gsub — find AND replace
`string.gsub(s, pattern, replacement)` returns a **new string** with every match swapped, plus a **count** of how many it changed. (Strings in Lua never change in place — you always get a new one back.)
~~~lua
local clean, n = string.gsub("a1b2c3", "%d", "#")
print(clean, n)  -- a#b#c#   3
~~~
Use `()` in the replacement too: `%1` means "the first capture".
~~~lua
print(string.gsub("neo@matrix", "(%a+)@(%a+)", "%2.%1"))  -- matrix.neo  1
~~~

## string.gmatch — loop over every match
`string.gmatch(s, pattern)` gives you an **iterator** — perfect for a `for` loop that visits each match one at a time.
~~~lua
for word in string.gmatch("red green blue", "%a+") do
  print(word)   -- red   green   blue (each on its own line)
end
~~~
With captures, `gmatch` hands you the captured pieces each time through the loop.

> INTEL — colon style works too: `s:match(p)` is the same as `string.match(s, p)`. Pick whichever reads better.

> WARNING — `..` joins strings (`"a" .. "b"` is `"ab"`). The `+` symbol is **only math** — `"a" + "b"` is an error. To build text, always use `..`, never `+`.

exercises.title: ID TAG
exercises.prompt:
Define **id_tag(name, lvl)** that returns a string built with **string.format** in the form `NAME [Lv.N]`, where N is a whole number.
~~~lua
id_tag("NEO", 7)   -- "NEO [Lv.7]"
id_tag("TRINITY", 12)  -- "TRINITY [Lv.12]"
~~~
Use the **%s** and **%d** specifiers.
exercises.starter:
function id_tag(name, lvl)
  -- TODO: use string.format with %s and %d
  return ""
end
exercises.lore: Every runner needs a callsign on the grid.

exercises.title: FIRST DIGIT
exercises.brief: Locate where the first run of digits starts.
exercises.prompt:
Define **first_digit_pos(s)** that returns the **1-indexed position** where the first digit appears in **s**, or **0** if there is no digit at all. Use **string.find** with the pattern `%d`.
~~~lua
first_digit_pos("id=A7")   -- 5
first_digit_pos("abc")     -- 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
function first_digit_pos(s)
  -- TODO: string.find(s, "%d") returns the start position, or nil
  return -1
end
exercises.solution:
function first_digit_pos(s)
  local i = string.find(s, "%d")
  if i then
    return i
  end
  return 0
end
exercises.hint: local i = string.find(s, "%d"); return i or 0
exercises.lore: Find the number buried in the noise.

exercises.title: PURE NUMERIC
exercises.brief: Is the whole string only digits?
exercises.prompt:
Define **all_digits(s)** that returns **true** if **s** is made up of one or more digits and NOTHING else, otherwise **false**. Use anchors: the pattern `^%d+$` matches a string that is entirely digits. An empty string is **false**.
~~~lua
all_digits("4242")   -- true
all_digits("4a2")    -- false
all_digits("")       -- false
~~~
exercises.starter:
function all_digits(s)
  -- TODO: match against ^%d+$ and return a boolean
  return true
end
exercises.lore: Clean keys only. Reject the tampered ones.

exercises.title: EXTRACT VALUE
exercises.brief: Pull the number out of a key=value pair.
exercises.prompt:
Define **grab_number(s)** that finds the first run of digits in **s** and returns it as a **number** (use tonumber), or **nil** if there are no digits. Match `%d+` so you grab the whole run, not just one digit.
~~~lua
grab_number("hp=255")   -- 255
grab_number("level 8 ok")  -- 8
grab_number("none")     -- nil
~~~
exercises.starter:
function grab_number(s)
  -- TODO: string.match(s, "%d+") then tonumber(...)
  return 0
end
exercises.solution:
function grab_number(s)
  local hit = string.match(s, "%d+")
  if hit then
    return tonumber(hit)
  end
  return nil
end
exercises.hint: local hit = string.match(s, "%d+"); return hit and tonumber(hit) or nil
exercises.lore: Strip the label, keep the payload.

exercises.title: SPLIT HANDLE
exercises.brief: Capture both sides of an @ handle.
exercises.prompt:
Define **split_handle(s)** for a handle like `user@host`. Use a pattern with two captures — `(%a+)@(%a+)` — and return the two captured strings (user first, host second).
~~~lua
local u, h = split_handle("neo@matrix")
-- u == "neo", h == "matrix"
~~~
exercises.starter:
function split_handle(s)
  -- TODO: return both captures from (%a+)@(%a+)
  return nil, nil
end
exercises.solution:
function split_handle(s)
  local user, host = string.match(s, "(%a+)@(%a+)")
  return user, host
end

tests.name: splits neo@matrix
tests.code: local u, h = split_handle("neo@matrix"); assert(u == "neo" and h == "matrix")

tests.name: splits root@zion
tests.code: local u, h = split_handle("root@zion"); assert(u == "root" and h == "zion")

exercises.lore: Two halves of one identity, decoded.

exercises.title: REDACT DIGITS
exercises.brief: Mask every digit and count them.
exercises.prompt:
Define **redact(s)** that replaces every digit in **s** with `#` using **string.gsub**, and returns BOTH the new string and the number of digits replaced (gsub already returns both values, in that order).
~~~lua
local out, n = redact("a1b2c3")
-- out == "a#b#c#", n == 3
~~~
exercises.starter:
function redact(s)
  -- TODO: use string.gsub(s, "%d", "#") and return both results
  return s, 0
end

tests.name: masks and counts
tests.code: local out, n = redact("a1b2c3"); assert(out == "a#b#c#" and n == 3)

tests.name: no digits
tests.code: local out, n = redact("abc"); assert(out == "abc" and n == 0)

tests.name: all digits
tests.code: local out, n = redact("007"); assert(out == "###" and n == 3)

exercises.hint: return string.gsub(s, "%d", "#")  -- gsub returns string AND count
exercises.lore: Scrub the serials before the dump leaks.

exercises.title: FLIP HANDLE
exercises.brief: Rewrite user@host as host.user using captures.
exercises.prompt:
Define **flip_handle(s)** that turns a handle `user@host` into `host.user`, using **string.gsub** with two captures `(%a+)@(%a+)` and a replacement that uses `%2` and `%1`. Return ONLY the rewritten string (gsub returns the count second — ignore it).
~~~lua
flip_handle("neo@matrix")  -- "matrix.neo"
~~~
exercises.starter:
function flip_handle(s)
  -- TODO: gsub with "(%a+)@(%a+)" -> "%2.%1", return only the string
  return s
end
exercises.solution:
function flip_handle(s)
  local out = string.gsub(s, "(%a+)@(%a+)", "%2.%1")
  return out
end

tests.name: returns a single string
tests.code: local r = flip_handle("a@b"); assert(r == "b.a" and type(r) == "string")

exercises.lore: Reverse the routing. Send the ghost home.

exercises.title: TALLY STREAM
exercises.brief: Add up every number found in the text.
exercises.prompt:
Define **sum_numbers(s)** that finds every run of digits in **s** and returns their total as a number. Loop with **string.gmatch(s, "%d+")**, convert each match with **tonumber**, and add it up. If there are no numbers, return **0**.
~~~lua
sum_numbers("a3 b10 c2")   -- 15
sum_numbers("none here")   -- 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
function sum_numbers(s)
  local total = 0
  -- TODO: for n in string.gmatch(s, "%d+") do ... end
  return total
end
exercises.solution:
function sum_numbers(s)
  local total = 0
  for n in string.gmatch(s, "%d+") do
    total = total + tonumber(n)
  end
  return total
end
exercises.hint: for n in string.gmatch(s, "%d+") do total = total + tonumber(n) end
exercises.lore: Drain every credit from the stream and tally it.
